subject: class_9th_social_science_part_1

payload.front: What is the main difference between a democracy and a non-democracy?
payload.back: A democracy is a form of government in which the rulers are elected by the people, whereas a non-democracy is a form of government in which the rulers are not elected by the people.

items.front: What is the significance of rights in a democracy?
items.back: Rights are necessary for the sustenance of a democracy. They protect minorities from the oppression of the majority and ensure that the majority cannot do whatever it likes.

items.front: What are rights?
items.back: Rights are claims of a person over other fellow beings, over the society and over the government. They are reasonable claims that are equally possible for others.

items.front: What is the rule of law?
items.back: The rule of law is the foundation of any democracy. It means that no person is above the law and that the laws apply in the same manner to all, regardless of a person's status.

items.front: What is the Right to Equality?
items.back: The Right to Equality means that the laws apply in the same manner to all, regardless of a person's status. It means that no person can be discriminated against or made ineligible for employment on the grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth.

items.front: What is the Right to Freedom?
items.back: The Right to Freedom means absence of constraints. It means absence of interference in our affairs by others - be it other individuals or the government. We want to live in society, but we want to be free.

items.front: What is the Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression?
items.back: The Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression means that we have the freedom to express our views and opinions without fear of persecution or reprisal. However, this freedom is not unlimited and can be restricted in certain circumstances.

items.front: What is the Right to Freedom of Religion?
items.back: The Right to Freedom of Religion means that every person has the right to profess, practice, and propagate their religion. However, this right does not mean that a person has the right to compel others to convert to their religion.

items.front: What is the Right to Constitutional Remedies?
items.back: The Right to Constitutional Remedies means that we have the right to seek remedy through courts in case of any violation of our Fundamental Rights. This right is also known as the 'heart and soul' of our Constitution.

items.front: What is the National Human Rights Commission?
items.back: The National Human Rights Commission is an independent commission set up by law to investigate and inquire into any case of human rights violation. It has the power to summon witnesses, question government officials, and demand official papers.

items.front: What is the scope of rights in India?
items.back: The scope of rights in India has expanded over the years. While Fundamental Rights are the source of all rights, our Constitution and law offers a wider range of rights. These include the right to freedom of press, right to information, and right to education.

items.front: What is the Right to Information Act?
items.back: The Right to Information Act gives citizens the right to seek information from government offices. It was enacted to promote transparency and accountability in government.

items.front: What is the Right to Education Act?
items.back: The Right to Education Act makes education a fundamental right for all children. It requires the government to provide free and compulsory education to all children up to the age of 14 years.

items.front: What is the Right to Food Act?
items.back: The Right to Food Act aims to provide food security to all citizens. It requires the government to ensure that all citizens have access to nutritious food.

items.front: What is the importance of the Right to Information Act?
items.back: The Right to Information Act is important because it promotes transparency and accountability in government. It allows citizens to seek information from government offices and hold them accountable for their actions.

items.front: What is the importance of the Right to Education Act?
items.back: The Right to Education Act is important because it makes education a fundamental right for all children. It requires the government to provide free and compulsory education to all children up to the age of 14 years.

items.front: What is the importance of the Right to Food Act?
items.back: The Right to Food Act is important because it aims to provide food security to all citizens. It requires the government to ensure that all citizens have access to nutritious food.

items.front: What is the role of the National Human Rights Commission?
items.back: The National Human Rights Commission plays a crucial role in protecting human rights in India. It investigates and inquires into any case of human rights violation and provides recommendations to the government.

items.front: What is the primary sector?
items.back: Primary sector refers to economic activities that involve the extraction of raw materials directly from nature, such as farming, fishing, and forestry.

items.front: What is the secondary sector?
items.back: Secondary sector refers to economic activities that involve the processing of raw materials derived from the primary sector into products for sale or consumption.

items.front: What is the tertiary sector?
items.back: Tertiary sector refers to economic activities that provide support to people involved in primary and secondary activities, such as transportation, banking, and management of business.

items.front: What are the three main types of economic sectors?
items.back: The three main types of economic sectors are primary, secondary, and tertiary sectors.

items.front: What is the difference between primary and secondary sectors?
items.back: Primary sector involves the extraction of raw materials from nature, while secondary sector involves the processing of raw materials into products.

items.front: How does the secondary sector depend on the tertiary sector?
items.back: The secondary sector depends on the tertiary sector for transportation, communication, and other services that facilitate the production and distribution of goods.

items.front: What is an example of interdependence between primary, secondary, and tertiary sectors?
items.back: An example of interdependence between primary, secondary, and tertiary sectors is the production of milk, where farmers (primary sector) supply milk to a dairy factory (secondary sector), which then sells the milk to consumers (tertiary sector).

items.front: What is the importance of the tertiary sector?
items.back: The tertiary sector is important because it provides support services that facilitate the production and distribution of goods, making it easier for people to access goods and services.

items.front: What is the role of the primary sector in the economy?
items.back: The primary sector plays a crucial role in the economy by providing raw materials that are essential for the production of goods and services.

items.front: What is the role of the secondary sector in the economy?
items.back: The secondary sector plays a crucial role in the economy by transforming raw materials into finished goods and services.

items.front: What is the role of the tertiary sector in the economy?
items.back: The tertiary sector plays a crucial role in the economy by providing support services that facilitate the production and distribution of goods and services.

items.front: What are some examples of primary sector activities?
items.back: Some examples of primary sector activities include farming, fishing, forestry, mining, and oil drilling.

items.front: What are some examples of secondary sector activities?
items.back: Some examples of secondary sector activities include manufacturing, construction, and transportation.

items.front: What are some examples of tertiary sector activities?
items.back: Some examples of tertiary sector activities include banking, communication, and tourism.

items.front: How do the three sectors interact with each other?
items.back: The three sectors interact with each other in a way that the primary sector provides raw materials to the secondary sector, which then transforms them into finished goods, and the tertiary sector provides support services to facilitate the production and distribution of goods.

items.front: What is the significance of the interdependence between the three sectors?
items.back: The interdependence between the three sectors is significant because it highlights the importance of each sector in the economy and how they work together to facilitate the production and distribution of goods and services.

items.front: What is a frequency polygon?

items.front: How do you draw a bar graph?
items.back: Represent the Heads (variable) on the horizontal axis, and the values of the variable on the vertical axis.

items.front: How do you draw a histogram?
items.back: Represent the class intervals on the horizontal axis, and the frequencies on the vertical axis.

items.front: How do you draw a frequency polygon?
items.back: Plot the class-marks along the horizontal axis, and the frequencies along the vertical-axis, and then plot and join the points by line segments.

items.front: What is the difference between a bar graph and a histogram?
items.back: A bar graph is used for discrete data, while a histogram is used for continuous data.

items.front: What is the difference between a histogram and a frequency polygon?
items.back: A histogram is a graphical representation of a grouped frequency distribution, while a frequency polygon is a visual way of representing quantitative data and its frequencies.

items.front: What is the purpose of a frequency polygon?
items.back: To compare two different sets of data of the same nature.

items.front: What is gravitation?
items.back: Gravitation is the force of attraction between two objects.

items.front: What is the universal law of gravitation?
items.back: The universal law of gravitation states that the force of attraction between two objects is proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

items.front: What is the gravitational force?
items.back: The gravitational force is the force of attraction between two objects.

items.front: What is the acceleration due to gravity?
items.back: The acceleration due to gravity is the acceleration of an object due to the gravitational force of the earth.

items.front: What is the weight of an object?
items.back: The weight of an object is the force with which the earth attracts it.

items.front: What is the difference between mass and weight?
items.back: Mass is a measure of the amount of matter in an object, while weight is the force exerted on an object by gravity.

items.front: What is the effect of thrust on different areas?
items.back: The effect of thrust on different areas depends on the area on which it acts.

items.front: What is pressure?
items.back: Pressure is the force per unit area acting on an object.

items.front: What is buoyancy?
items.back: Buoyancy is the upward force exerted by a fluid on an object.

items.front: What is Archimedes' principle?
items.back: Archimedes' principle states that the buoyant force on an object is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by it.

items.front: What is the force of gravitation between two objects?
items.back: The force of gravitation between two objects is proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

items.front: What is the effect of distance on the force of gravitation?
items.back: The force of gravitation decreases with distance.

items.front: What is the effect of mass on the force of gravitation?
items.back: The force of gravitation increases with mass.

items.front: What is the effect of acceleration on the force of gravitation?
items.back: The force of gravitation is independent of acceleration.

items.front: What is the difference between the weight of an object on the earth and the moon?
items.back: The weight of an object on the moon is 1/6 of its weight on the earth.

items.front: What is the effect of thrust on a fluid?
items.back: The effect of thrust on a fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions.

items.front: What is the effect of buoyancy on an object?
items.back: The effect of buoyancy on an object is to exert an upward force.

items.front: What is the principle of buoyancy?
items.back: The principle of buoyancy states that the buoyant force on an object is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by it.

items.front: What is the effect of the density of a fluid on buoyancy?
items.back: The effect of the density of a fluid on buoyancy is that the buoyant force increases with the density of the fluid.

items.front: What is the effect of the density of an object on buoyancy?
items.back: The effect of the density of an object on buoyancy is that the buoyant force decreases with the density of the object.

items.front: What is the effect of the volume of a fluid on buoyancy?
items.back: The effect of the volume of a fluid on buoyancy is that the buoyant force increases with the volume of the fluid.

items.front: What is the effect of the volume of an object on buoyancy?
items.back: The effect of the volume of an object on buoyancy is that the buoyant force decreases with the volume of the object.

items.front: What is matter?
items.back: Matter is everything around us, made up of particles that occupy space and have mass.

items.front: What are the three states of matter?
items.back: Solid, liquid, and gas.

items.front: What is the difference between solid and liquid?
items.back: Solids have a fixed shape and volume, while liquids have a fixed volume but take the shape of their container.

items.front: What is the difference between liquid and gas?
items.back: Liquids have a fixed volume but take the shape of their container, while gases have neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume.

items.front: What is the effect of temperature on the state of matter?
items.back: Increasing temperature can change the state of matter from solid to liquid to gas.

items.front: What is the effect of pressure on the state of matter?
items.back: Increasing pressure can change the state of matter from gas to liquid to solid.

items.front: What is evaporation?
items.back: The process by which a liquid changes into a gas.

items.front: What is sublimation?
items.back: The process by which a solid changes directly into a gas.

items.front: What is deposition?
items.back: The process by which a gas changes directly into a solid.

items.front: What is the difference between boiling and evaporation?
items.back: Boiling is a bulk phenomenon, while evaporation is a surface phenomenon.

items.front: What is latent heat of vaporization?
items.back: The heat energy required to change 1 kg of a liquid to gas at atmospheric pressure at its boiling point.

items.front: What is latent heat of fusion?
items.back: The amount of heat energy required to change 1 kg of solid into liquid at its melting point.

items.front: What is the effect of humidity on evaporation?
items.back: High humidity decreases the rate of evaporation.

items.front: What is the effect of wind speed on evaporation?
items.back: High wind speed increases the rate of evaporation.

items.front: What is the effect of surface area on evaporation?
items.back: Increasing surface area increases the rate of evaporation.

items.front: What is the effect of temperature on evaporation?
items.back: Increasing temperature increases the rate of evaporation.

items.front: What is the physical state of water at 0°C?
items.back: Solid (ice).

items.front: What is the physical state of water at 100°C?
items.back: Gas (water vapour).

items.front: What is the physical state of water at 25°C?
items.back: Liquid.

items.front: What is the difference between solid and gas?
items.back: Solids have a fixed shape and volume, while gases have neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume.

items.back: Increasing pressure can change the state of matter from gas to liquid to solid.

payload.back: A mixture is a physical combination of two or more substances.

items.front: What is the law of conservation of mass?
items.back: The law of conservation of mass states that mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.

items.front: What is the law of definite proportions?
items.back: The law of definite proportions states that elements are always present in a definite proportion by mass in a compound.

items.front: What is an atom?
items.back: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that cannot usually exist independently and retain all its chemical properties.

items.front: What is a molecule?
items.back: A molecule is the smallest particle of an element or a compound capable of independent existence under ordinary conditions.

items.front: What is a chemical formula?
items.back: A chemical formula is a symbolic representation of the composition of a compound.

items.front: What is the atomic mass unit?
items.back: The atomic mass unit is a unit of mass equal to 1/12th the mass of one carbon-12 atom.

items.front: What is the formula unit mass?

items.front: What is the difference between a molecule and a formula unit?
items.back: A molecule is the smallest particle of an element or a compound, while a formula unit is the smallest particle of an ionic compound.

chapter_title: Chapter 5 The Fundamental Unit of Life
subject: science

items.front: What is the fundamental unit of life?
items.back: The cell is the fundamental unit of life.

items.front: Who discovered cells?
items.back: Robert Hooke discovered cells in 1665.

items.front: What is the cell membrane?
items.back: The cell membrane is a selectively permeable membrane that separates the cell from its external environment.

items.front: What is osmosis?
items.back: Osmosis is the movement of water across a selectively permeable membrane from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration.

items.front: What is the nucleus?
items.back: The nucleus is the control center of the cell that contains the genetic material.

items.front: What is the endoplasmic reticulum?
items.back: The endoplasmic reticulum is a network of membrane-bound tubes and sheets that is involved in protein synthesis and transport.

items.front: What is the Golgi apparatus?
items.back: The Golgi apparatus is a complex of membrane-bound vesicles that is involved in the storage, modification, and packaging of substances.

items.front: What is the function of lysosomes?
items.back: Lysosomes are involved in the digestion and breakdown of cellular waste and foreign substances.

items.front: What is the function of mitochondria?
items.back: Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell and are involved in the generation of energy.

items.front: What is the difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.back: Prokaryotic cells lack a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, while eukaryotic cells have a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.

items.front: What is the process of cell division?
items.back: Cell division is the process by which a cell divides to form two daughter cells.

items.front: What is the difference between mitosis and meiosis?
items.back: Mitosis is the process of cell division that results in two daughter cells with the same number of chromosomes, while meiosis is the process of cell division that results in four daughter cells with half the number of chromosomes.

items.front: What is the function of the cell wall?
items.back: The cell wall provides structural support and protection to the cell.

items.front: What is the difference between plant and animal cells?
items.back: Plant cells have a cell wall and chloroplasts, while animal cells do not have a cell wall or chloroplasts.

items.front: What is the function of the plasma membrane?
items.back: The plasma membrane regulates the movement of materials between the cell and its external environment.

items.front: What is the difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells in terms of size?
items.back: Prokaryotic cells are generally smaller than eukaryotic cells.

items.front: What is the function of the Golgi apparatus in terms of storage?
items.back: The Golgi apparatus is involved in the storage of substances.

items.front: What is the function of mitochondria in terms of energy generation?
items.back: Mitochondria generate energy for the cell through the process of cellular respiration.

items.front: What is the difference between chromoplasts and leucoplasts?
items.back: Chromoplasts are involved in photosynthesis, while leucoplasts are involved in storage.

items.front: What is the function of the nuclear membrane?
items.back: The nuclear membrane regulates the movement of materials between the nucleus and the cytoplasm.

items.front: What is the difference between the ER and the Golgi apparatus?
items.back: The ER is involved in protein synthesis and transport, while the Golgi apparatus is involved in the storage, modification, and packaging of substances.

items.front: What is the function of lysosomes in terms of digestion?
items.back: Lysosomes are involved in the digestion and breakdown of cellular waste and foreign substances.

items.front: What is the difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells in terms of genetic material?
items.back: Prokaryotic cells have only nucleic acid as genetic material, while eukaryotic cells have DNA and proteins as genetic material.

items.front: What is the function of the cell wall in terms of structural support?
items.back: The cell wall provides structural support and protection to the cell.

items.front: What is the difference between plant and animal cells in terms of organelles?
items.back: Plant cells have chloroplasts, while animal cells do not have chloroplasts.

items.front: What is the function of the plasma membrane in terms of regulation?
items.back: The plasma membrane regulates the movement of materials between the cell and its external environment.

items.front: What is the difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells in terms of size and complexity?
items.back: Prokaryotic cells are generally smaller and less complex than eukaryotic cells.

items.front: What is a half?
items.back: A half is one of two equal parts of a whole.

items.back: Two halves make one whole.

items.front: What is a quarter?
items.back: A quarter is one of four equal parts of a whole.

items.front: How many quarters make one whole?
items.back: Four quarters make one whole.
